heading: CHE INSEGNA A TUTTI E ILLUMINA TUTTE LE COSE
card_summary: This section of Zorzi's De Harmonia Mundi, belonging to the Secondo Cantico (Quarto Tono), develops a sustained Christological argument structured around the Hebrew word emet (truth) and its kabbalistic numerology. Zorzi identifies Christ as the Alpha and Omega — the Alef and Taw in Hebrew — who unites beginning, middle, and end in himself, and proceeds to demonstrate that every jot and tittle of the Mosaic law and prophetic scripture is fulfilled in him. Drawing on Rabbi Simeon ben Yochay, the kabbalist Elcana, Pythagorean numerology, and the Church Fathers (Jerome, Augustine, Paul, John), Zorzi performs an extended letter-by-letter analysis of the opening word of Genesis (Bereshit) to show Trinitarian doctrine encoded in Hebrew letters, and deploys the technique of tzeruf (letter-permutation) to extract the name Yeshu (Jesus) from Genesis 49:10, Psalm 71, and Psalm 95.
essay:
# CHE INSEGNA A TUTTI E ILLUMINA TUTTE LE COSE
## (Secondo Cantico, Quarto Tono — pp. 1551–1644)

*Note: This summary covers the opening portion of this long section (approximately the first 500 lines of the file, corresponding to chapters 1–4 of the section, pages roughly 1384–1401 of the Campanini edition). The full section extends considerably further.*

---

## 1. The Definition of Truth (Verità / Emet)

Zorzi opens with a philosophical definition of truth as "la rettitudine che si percepisce con la sola mente" — a rectitude perceptible by the mind alone — that derives from the "concordia e corrispondenza del termine medio con il principio e la fine." He then grounds this philosophical definition in Hebrew philology. The Hebrew word for truth, **emet** (אמת), is composed of three letters: Alef (א), the first letter of the alphabet; Mem (מ), the middle letter; and Taw (ת), the last letter. This tripartite structure encodes the structure of reality itself: emanation from the beginning, traversal through the middle, and return to the end.

> "In essa convergono unite insieme la prima lettera dell'alfabeto, la media e l'ultima. Infatti א, alef, è la prima, ת, taw, l'ultima e מ, mem, quella di mezzo: le tre insieme, vocalizzate con i punti opportuni, si pronunciano emet."

<cite>p. 1384</cite>

Zorzi extends this into gematria (numerical letter-value analysis): Alef = 1 and 1000 (the cube of ten, the complete number); Mem = 40 (ten multiplied by four, "il numero radicale e il solido fondamento di tutti i numeri"); Taw = 400 (ten times forty, or four times one hundred). These values are "solidi" — stable and cubic — pointing to divine, celestial, and terrestrial realities respectively. By contrast, the Hebrew word for falsehood, **sheqer** (שקר), is composed of Qof (100), Resh (200), and Shin (300), all hundreds, all associated with "le realtà inferiori e grossolane." The letters of sheqer are also morphologically unstable: they cannot stand upright without support, literalising the proverb "le bugie hanno le gambe corte."

---

## 2. Christ as Truth (Caput II)

The Christological move is immediate and decisive. Christ is truth because he himself is beginning, middle, and end — not merely the principle that aligns them:

> "Non solo egli fa corrispondere il termine medio con il principio e con il fine, ma lui stesso è principio, mezzo e fine, come attesta egli stesso parlando a Giovanni nella visione di Patmos: Io sono l'α e l'ω, l'alfa e l'omega, il principio e la fine."

<cite>p. 1385</cite>

Zorzi notes that Christ spoke in Greek (and the Apocalypse was written in Greek), but that the statement is equivalent in Hebrew: "Io sono א, alef e ת, taw." Christ incarnated the human form — "il termine medio tra Dio e tutte le altre creature" — precisely in order to perform this cosmic function, binding all created things to their divine origin. He is therefore not only "la via," but also "porta, vite, tronco, canone, dottore, maestro, regolatore," citing John 1:14 ("pieno di grazia e verità") and John 1:16 ("dalla cui pienezza noi tutti abbiamo ricevuto").

---

## 3. Christ as the Truth of the Law (Caput III)

Zorzi argues that all of Scripture — law and prophets alike — is oriented toward Christ as its fulfillment and verification. He cites John 1:17 ("La legge fu data per mezzo di Mosè, la grazia e la verità furono compiute per mezzo di Gesù Cristo"), Phillip's identification of Jesus in John 1:45, and Christ's own claim in John 5:46 ("Mosè ha scritto di me"). He distinguishes several modes of Christological reference in Scripture: genealogy (from Adam through the Pentateuch, citing both Matthew and Luke, whose apparent discrepancies Jerome and Augustine explain as natural versus legal genealogies); typological/figural readings (the tabernacle, sacrifices, the priesthood of Melchisedec and Aaron interpreted by Paul in Hebrews); historical effects wrought by the power of the coming Christ (the leathern garments given to Adam and Eve; the liberation of Abraham from Ur; water from the rock, which Paul explicitly calls Christ); and direct prophecy (Isaiah 9:6; Genesis 49:10; the Psalms, especially Psalms 71 and 88).

A notable eschatological note surfaces here: Zorzi identifies the ongoing persecution of "i coeredi di Cristo" by the Ishmaelites (i.e., Muslims, the sons of the handmaid) with Paul's allegory in Galatians 4, and states with remarkable confidence: "Quel tempo (se non mi sbaglio) è vicinissimo" — the age when there will be "un solo gregge e un solo pastore."

---

## 4. The iota and the Apex: Kabbalistic Analysis of Bereshit (Caput IV)

The most technically elaborated section reads the opening word of Genesis, **Bereshit** (בראשית), letter by letter, following the method of Rabbi Simeon ben Yochay and the kabbalist Elcana (identified as authorities on the mystical significance of letter-forms and their cantillation marks):

- **Bet (ב, = 2)**: "Bet significa casa e indica la sapienza superiore" — the divine Wisdom (Hokhmah) in which all things pre-existed, equated with John's Logos. As the first letter, it points to the Son as the second person of the Trinity, the first production in the divine sphere.
- **Resh (ר, = 200)**: Signifies the first created thing in the material order, namely prime matter, which the Pythagoreans symbolise by duality.
- **Alef (א)**: "La lettera della divinità e della prima Sorgente di tutti i beni" — the Giver of Forms who acts upon bet and resh, enlivening matter through the Son. Crucially: "alef, unito a bet, forma la parola אב, av, che vuol dire 'padre', e in quanto padre non può essere senza figlio."
- **Shin (ש, = 300)**: The Holy Spirit as the first Giver of Forms, impressing form on matter — the Trinitarian third.
- **Yod (י)**: The divine fullness infused into all things, citing Jeremiah 23:24 ("Io riempio il cielo e la terra") and Colossians 1:19 ("è piaciuto al Padre far abitare in lui ogni pienezza"). Yod begins the name of Jesus (ישו, Yeshu).
- **Taw (ת)**: "Significa la comunità di Israele" (citing Elcana) — the congregation of the elect, gathered into the first word of creation.

> "Il significato complessivo di tutte quelle lettere è il seguente: 'La materia prima di tutte le cose è stata creata con sapienza, che è il Verbo e il Figlio del sommo Padre, in seguito è stata conferita alle cose la forma dalla Sorgente di tutti i beni e, di conseguenza, tutte sono completate dalla bontà'."

<cite>p. 1399</cite>

---

## 5. Tzeruf (Letter-Permutation) and the Name of Jesus

Zorzi then introduces the technique of **tzeruf** (צירוף, letter-permutation/anagram), used to extract hidden names from Scripture. Taking the Hebrew word chamor (חמור, 'donkey' — the animal Christ rode on Palm Sunday), he permutes the last letter to the front to obtain **rechem** (רחם, 'mercy/womb/compassion'), interpreting Christ's entry into Jerusalem as the beginning of the supreme act of compassion.

More elaborately, Zorzi extracts the acrostic name **Yeshu (ישו)** from three key prophetic texts by taking the initial letters of successive words:

- **Genesis 49:10**: The prophecy of Jacob to Judah, "Iabo siloh velo" (יבא שלה ולו) — first letters Yod, Shin, Vav = ישו.
- **Psalm 71:17**: "iinon semo veitbarcu bo col goim" — first letters again spell ישו, demonstrating that the name of the one "in whom all nations shall be blessed" is Jesus.
- **Psalm 95**: Zorzi notes the name is encoded "con mistero ancora più celebre" in the psalm inviting a new song for the Messiah's new work.

> "Perciò non senza ragione proprio la lettera yod è posta all'inizio del nome ישו, Yeshu."

<cite>p. 1400</cite>

---

## Cosmological and Theological Connections

Throughout, Zorzi weaves together several threads central to his larger project in *De Harmonia Mundi*: the structural identity of Neoplatonic emanation (One → Intellect → Soul) with Trinitarian theology (Father → Son/Logos → Holy Spirit); the kabbalistic encoding of this triad in Hebrew letter-values and letter-forms; and the Christological concentration of all cosmic harmonics in the person of Jesus as the mediating Word. The citation of Hermes Trismegistus ("Mercurio") alongside Paul, John, and the Hebrew kabbalists is characteristic of Zorzi's syncretist method. The identification of the Pythagorean dyad with prime matter, and the interpretation of Hebrew letter-shapes as cosmological diagrams, reflects Zorzi's investment in "la vera matematica formale" as a universal key to Scripture and nature alike.
